title: Rosemary Focaccia

subs.name: half maida and half atta (whole wheat flour)
subs.notes: the texture will be denser and more wholesome; add 20 ml extra water as atta absorbs more

subs.name: 2 tsp baking powder mixed into the flour — skip the proofing and rest steps
subs.notes: the bread will be more cake-like and less chewy; reduce oven time by 5 minutes

subs.name: dried thyme, oregano or carom seeds (ajwain)
subs.notes: ajwain gives an unusual but pleasant Indian-Italian twist

subs.name: sun-dried tomato pieces or sliced jalapeño
subs.notes: sun-dried tomatoes intensify the flavour; jalapeño adds heat

steps.text: Dissolve the yeast and sugar in 100 ml of the warm water. Leave for 5–8 minutes until it becomes frothy — this confirms the yeast is active.

Combine flour and salt in a large bowl. Pour in the yeast mixture, remaining water and 3 tablespoons of the olive oil. Mix until a sticky dough forms, then knead on a floured surface for 8–10 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. The dough should be wetter than bread dough — resist adding too much flour.
Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth and rest in a warm spot for 60 minutes until roughly doubled in size.

Pour 3 tablespoons of olive oil into a 30 × 20 cm baking tray and tilt to coat the base generously. Transfer the risen dough to the tray and stretch it gently to fill the tin. Cover and rest for 20 minutes — this allows the gluten to relax so you can stretch it fully without it springing back.

Preheat the oven to 220 °C. Dimple the dough aggressively all over with your fingertips, pressing all the way to the base of the tray. Drizzle the remaining olive oil generously into the dimples — it will pool there and form crispy pockets.

Scatter the rosemary and olives over the top. Sprinkle with a little extra salt. Bake for 20–25 minutes until deeply golden on top and the sides have pulled away from the tray. The bottom should sound hollow when tapped.
Remove from the oven, drizzle over one final tablespoon of olive oil while hot, and allow to cool in the tray for 10 minutes before cutting into squares or rectangles.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Fermentation conditions affect safety. Discard batches with unexpected mould, putrid odour or other spoilage signs.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rosemary → dried thyme, oregano or carom seeds (ajwain) (ajwain gives an unusual but pleasant Indian-Italian twist); olives → sun-dried tomato pieces or sliced jalapeño (sun-dried tomatoes intensify the flavour; jalapeño adds heat); yeast → 2 tsp baking powder mixed into the flour — skip the proofing and rest steps (the bread will be more cake-like and less chewy; reduce oven time by 5 minutes); maida → half maida and half atta (whole wheat flour) (the texture will be denser and more wholesome; add 20 ml extra water as atta absorbs more)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: An oven is needed for authentic focaccia. Without one, heat a heavy iron tawa on medium-low, place the dimpled dough on it, cover tightly with a lid and cook for 8–10 minutes on the lowest flame until the base is golden. Finish by placing a piece of burning charcoal inside an inverted steel katori set on top of the dough and cover for 3 minutes to brown the top. Maida is available at every kirana store. Dried rosemary is in the spice aisle of most supermarkets.
